questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory investigation is considered the most sensitive and specific early indicator of depleted iron stores in iron deficiency anemia?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels reflect total body iron stores and are the first to decrease when iron stores are being depleted. Option A can fluctuate due to dietary intake or inflammation. Option B increases in later stages of iron deficiency but is less specific. Option C only drops once functional iron is exhausted. Option E decreases but is generally less reliable than ferritin for diagnosing store depletion.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55 year old strict vegan presents with tingling sensations in his feet and a smooth, beefy red tongue. Peripheral blood smear reveals macro-ovalocytes. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neurological symptoms like tingling) and glossitis. Option A causes microcytic anemia without neurological symptoms. Option C causes megaloblastic anemia but typically does not present with neurological deficits. Option D causes scurvy. Option E is associated with sideroblastic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 10 year old boy with an acute pyogenic bacterial infection is expected to show which of the following changes in his white blood cell count?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is a reactive leukocytosis typically seen in response to acute bacterial infections and tissue necrosis. Option A is characteristic of viral infections. Option B is associated with parasitic infections and allergic reactions. Option D is seen in chronic infections like tuberculosis. Option E is rare but can be seen in myeloproliferative disorders.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the emergency department, a patient requires an immediate blood transfusion before cross-matching can be completed. Which blood group is considered the universal donor for packed red blood cells?
questions.explanation: O negative blood has neither A, B, nor Rh antigens on the red cell surface, making it safe for transfusion into any recipient in an emergency. Option A is the universal recipient for plasma. Option B can only be given to Rh positive recipients. Option C is the universal recipient for red cells. Option E contains A antigens and would cause hemolysis in B or O group recipients.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a hematopoietic growth factor essential for the production of red blood cells. Which organ is the primary site of its production in adults?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin is primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidneys in response to hypoxia. Option A is the primary site of production in the fetus but only a minor site in adults. Option B is involved in blood filtration and lymphocyte storage. Option C is the site of action for erythropoietin, not its production. Option E is involved in T-cell maturation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5 year old girl presents with multiple petechiae and easy bruising. Which initial laboratory test should be performed to evaluate the role of platelets in this bleeding disorder?
questions.explanation: Petechiae and mucosal bleeding suggest a defect in primary hemostasis, which is assessed by platelet count and function. Option A assesses the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation. Option B assesses the intrinsic and common pathways. Option D assesses the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Option E measures the quantity of fibrinogen available for clot formation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following RBC indices indicates the average concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red blood cells?
questions.explanation: MCHC measures the concentration of hemoglobin in a deciliter of packed red cells. Option A measures the average size of red cells. Option B measures the average mass of hemoglobin per red cell. Option D measures the variation in red cell size (anisocytosis). Option E indicates the rate of new red cell production by the bone marrow.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction most commonly occurs due to ABO incompatibility. What is the immunological mechanism involved?
questions.explanation: Type II hypersensitivity involves antibodies (IgM or IgG) binding to antigens on the cell surface (donor RBCs), leading to complement activation and intravascular hemolysis. Option A involves IgE and mast cells. Option C involves immune complex deposition. Option D is a cell-mediated delayed response. Option E occurs when donor T-cells attack recipient tissues.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with generalized itching after a hot bath and a high red cell mass. A peripheral smear shows an absolute increase in all three cell lines. What is the most likely benign reactive cause for eosinophilia specifically?
questions.explanation: Bronchial asthma, along with other allergic conditions and parasitic infections, is a common cause of reactive eosinophilia. Option A is a malignant cause of leukocytosis. Option B typically causes lymphocytosis. Option D and E are disorders of red cells and do not typically cause eosinophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old pregnant female presents with severe microcytic hypochromic anemia. Which of the following findings on her peripheral blood smear would be most characteristic of iron deficiency?
questions.explanation: Pencil-shaped or elongated red cells are classic findings in iron deficiency anemia alongside microcytosis and hypochromia. Option A and B are seen in megaloblastic anemias. Option D can be seen in thalassemia or liver disease. Option E indicates microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old male with a history of chronic alcoholism presents with macrocytic anemia. A bone marrow biopsy is performed. Which finding would most strongly support a diagnosis of megaloblastic anemia?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony, where the nucleus matures more slowly than the cytoplasm due to impaired DNA synthesis. Option A is typical of aplastic anemia. Option C is seen in anemia of chronic disease or sideroblastic anemia. Option D is the hallmark of sideroblastic anemia. Option E is seen in amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Filgrastim (G-CSF) is used to treat neutropenia in patients undergoing chemotherapy. Which of the following is a commonly reported adverse effect of this hematopoietic growth factor?
questions.explanation: Bone pain is a frequent side effect of G-CSF therapy, thought to be due to the expansion of the marrow as it produces neutrophils. Option A is an adverse effect more commonly associated with Erythropoietin. Option C and D are not classic adverse effects of G-CSF. Option E is not typically linked to this growth factor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A newborn develops severe jaundice and hydrops fetalis shortly after birth. The mother is Rh-negative and the father is Rh-positive. Which mechanism explains this condition?
questions.explanation: In Rh incompatibility, maternal IgG antibodies (produced after sensitization in a previous pregnancy) cross the placenta and destroy fetal Rh-positive red cells. Option A is usually milder as ABO antigens are poorly developed in newborns. Option C is incorrect because IgM does not cross the placenta. Option D refers to transfusion reactions. Option E is an intrinsic RBC defect not related to blood grouping.

options.B: Maternal IgG antibodies crossing the placenta
options.C: Maternal IgM antibodies crossing the placenta
options.E: Deficiency of G6PD in the neonatal red cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12 year old boy presents with painful swelling of the knee joint after minor trauma. Lab tests show a prolonged aPTT but a normal PT and normal platelet count. Which bleeding disorder is most likely?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) typically presents with hemarthrosis and a prolonged aPTT, while PT and platelet count remain normal. Option A often shows prolonged bleeding time alongside aPTT. Option B would prolong both PT and aPTT. Option D would involve abnormalities in PT, aPTT, and platelet count. Option E presents with an isolated low platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with anemia of chronic disease, the serum iron is low despite adequate bone marrow iron stores. This is primarily mediated by the upregulation of which protein?
questions.explanation: Hepcidin is an acute-phase reactant produced by the liver that inhibits ferroportin, preventing iron release from macrophages and absorption from the gut. Option A is usually inappropriately low or normal in this condition. Option B is downregulated by hepcidin. Option D levels are typically decreased in anemia of chronic disease. Option E is the protein shell of ferritin, not the regulator of iron release.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old male presents with a total WBC count of 55,000/microL. You need to differentiate between a reactive leukemoid reaction and chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Which finding would strongly suggest a leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation: A high LAP score indicates that the neutrophils are mature and functional, typical of a reactive process like a leukemoid reaction. Option A is the genetic hallmark of CML. Option B is characteristic of CML where the malignant cells are enzymatically deficient. Option D and E are classic clinical and lab features of CML.

options.D: Presence of a large number of basophils

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by macrocytosis, but not all macrocytic anemias are megaloblastic. Which of the following conditions causes a non-megaloblastic macrocytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Liver disease and chronic alcoholism can cause macrocytosis without megaloblastic changes (no hypersegmented neutrophils or megaloblasts in marrow). Options A, B, D, and E all interfere with DNA synthesis, leading to true megaloblastic changes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with lifelong mucosal bleeding. Laboratory testing shows a normal platelet count but failure of platelets to aggregate in response to Ristocetin, which is not corrected by adding normal plasma. What is the defective component?
questions.explanation: Failure of Ristocetin-induced aggregation that does not correct with normal plasma suggests Bernard-Soulier Syndrome, caused by a deficiency of GPIb. Option A is deficient in Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Option C (vWF deficiency) would correct with normal plasma as the plasma provides the missing vWF. Option D deficiency is Hemophilia B. Option E is involved in platelet activation but not the primary binding to vWF.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the primary regulator of megakaryocyte maturation and platelet production. Which of the following is the main site of TPO synthesis?
questions.explanation: While produced in small amounts in the kidneys and marrow, the liver is the primary constitutive source of thrombopoietin. Option A and B are minor contributors. Option D is the primary site for Erythropoietin. Option E possesses the receptors (MPL) for TPO but is not the primary site of synthesis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her lab reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following conditions is the most likely cause of this finding?
questions.explanation: An MCV below 80 fL indicates microcytic anemia, of which iron deficiency is the most common cause. Option A and B are incorrect because they cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). Option D is incorrect as it typically presents as normocytic anemia. Option E is incorrect because it is usually normocytic but characterized by a high MCHC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a peripheral blood smear, the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) is a hallmark feature of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils occur due to impaired DNA synthesis in megaloblastic anemias (B12 or Folate deficiency). Option A is incorrect as IDA shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Option C is incorrect as CML shows a shift to the left with immature precursors. Option D and E are incorrect because infections typically show toxic granulation or a shift to the left, not hypersegmentation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory parameter is the most sensitive and earliest indicator of depleted iron stores in the body?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the first lab value to decrease in iron deficiency. Option A is incorrect as serum iron fluctuates and stays normal until stores are gone. Option B and E are incorrect because they only decrease in the final stage of anemia. Option C is incorrect as TIBC increases as a compensatory mechanism but is less specific than ferritin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient’s lab report shows a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with an absolute eosinophil count of 1,200/mm3. This reactive leukocytosis is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a classic reactive response to parasites and allergic reactions. Option A is incorrect as bacterial infections cause neutrophilia. Option C and E are incorrect because tissue necrosis and trauma typically cause neutrophilia. Option D is incorrect as blood loss primarily affects the erythroid line.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the primary screening test used to evaluate the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic pathway (Factor VII) and common pathway. Option A is incorrect as it measures platelet function and vessel wall integrity. Option B is incorrect because APTT measures the intrinsic pathway. Option D is incorrect as it specifically measures fibrinogen to fibrin conversion. Option E is incorrect as it is an obsolete test for platelet function.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The term thrombocytopenia refers to a platelet count below which threshold?
questions.explanation: Thrombocytopenia is defined as a platelet count less than 150,000/mm3. Option A and B are within the normal range (150,000 to 450,000). Option D and E are incorrect because while they represent degrees of thrombocytopenia, they are not the defining upper threshold.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by the sequestering of iron in macrophages. Which molecule mediates this process by inhibiting iron release?
questions.explanation: Hepcidin is an acute-phase reactant that blocks ferroportin, preventing iron release from storage. Option A is incorrect as transferrin is a transport protein. Option C is incorrect because ferroportin is the channel being blocked, not the mediator. Option D is incorrect as it stimulates RBC production. Option E is incorrect because it is a breakdown product of heme.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In blood grouping, the Landsteiner's Law states that if an agglutinogen is present in the red cells, the corresponding agglutinin must be:
questions.explanation: According to Landsteiner, the corresponding antibody must be absent from the serum to prevent auto-agglutination. Option A is incorrect as that would cause hemolysis. Options C and D are incorrect as blood group antigens and antibodies are related to RBCs and plasma/serum, not WBCs. Option E is incorrect as these are naturally occurring antibodies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old man presents with fatigue and numbness in his feet. Lab tests reveal a macrocytic anemia (MCV 115 fL). Which of the following findings would specifically favor Vitamin B12 deficiency over Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Both deficiencies raise homocysteine, but only B12 deficiency raises MMA because B12 is a cofactor for converting methylmalonyl CoA to succinyl CoA. Options A, B, D, and E are seen in both Folate and B12 deficiencies and cannot distinguish between the two.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following peripheral smear findings is most characteristic of Thalassemia minor, helping to distinguish it from Iron Deficiency Anemia?
questions.explanation: Thalassemia often shows target cells and basophilic stippling (due to precipitated RNA), whereas IDA usually does not. Option A and C are features of macrocytic anemias. Option D is incorrect as schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Option E is incorrect as spherocytes indicate hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with a history of Rheumatoid Arthritis presents with normochromic normocytic anemia. Lab results show low serum iron, low TIBC, and high serum ferritin. What is the mechanism of her anemia?
questions.explanation: This is Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD). Inflammation (IL-6) increases hepcidin, which traps iron in macrophages (high ferritin) and reduces serum iron and TIBC. Options A, B, and D describe IDA, which would show low ferritin and high TIBC. Option E describes hemolytic anemia, which would show high LDH and bilirubin.

options.A: Chronic blood loss from the GI tract
options.B: Reduced iron absorption in the duodenum
options.C: Cytokine-induced inhibition of erythropoiesis and iron trapping

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction due to ABO incompatibility, what is the primary mechanism of red cell destruction?
questions.explanation: ABO antibodies (IgM) are potent complement activators, causing immediate intravascular hemolysis. Option A is Type IV hypersensitivity. Option B is Type I hypersensitivity (allergic). Option D is cellular immunity. Option E is characteristic of Rh incompatibility or minor antigen reactions (extravascular hemolysis).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with mucosal bleeding. The lab results show: Platelet count 15,000/mm3, PT 12 sec (Normal 11-13), and APTT 30 sec (Normal 25-35). What is the most likely classification of this bleeding disorder?
questions.explanation: The normal PT and APTT rule out pathway deficiencies or DIC, and the very low platelet count explains the bleeding. Option A is incorrect because PT/APTT are normal. Option C is incorrect as DIC would show prolonged PT/APTT and low fibrinogen. Option D is incorrect as vWD usually has a prolonged bleeding time and sometimes a long APTT. Option E is incorrect as Hemophilia A presents with a long APTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Filgrastim is a recombinant hematopoietic growth factor used clinically to treat chemotherapy-induced cytopenia. What is its specific mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: Filgrastim is a G-CSF analog that specifically increases neutrophil production. Option A describes the action of Thrombopoietin or Oprelvekin (IL-11). Option B describes the action of Erythropoietin. Option D is a theoretical treatment for ACD. Option E describes multi-CSF or different interleukins.

options.B: Stimulates the JAK2 pathway in erythroid precursors
options.C: Acts on the G-CSF receptor to increase neutrophil production
options.D: Inhibits hepcidin synthesis in the liver

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old male presents with pancytopenia. A bone marrow biopsy reveals a "dry tap" and is found to be hypocellular, consisting mostly of fat cells and stroma. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by pancytopenia and a hypocellular bone marrow replaced by fat. Option A is incorrect as myelofibrosis would show extensive collagen/fibrosis and teardrop cells. Option B is incorrect as leukemia would show a hypercellular marrow filled with blasts. Option D and E are incorrect because the marrow is usually hypercellular due to erythroid hyperplasia trying to compensate for the anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old boy has a history of recurrent painful joint swellings after minor trauma. Laboratory findings show: PT: 12 sec (Normal), APTT: 75 sec (Normal 25-35), Bleeding Time: 4 min (Normal), Platelet Count: 250,000/mm3. This profile is most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) causes a prolonged APTT with normal PT, BT, and platelet count. Option A and E are incorrect as they would involve the extrinsic pathway and prolong PT. Option B is incorrect as vWD typically prolongs bleeding time. Option D is incorrect because ITP would show low platelets and normal APTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of leukemias versus reactive leukocytosis, the "Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score" is often used. A very low LAP score is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Mature neutrophils in CML are biochemically abnormal and lack LAP, distinguishing it from a leukemoid reaction (high LAP score). Option A, D, and E typically show elevated LAP scores. Option B involves lymphoblasts which are not evaluated by the LAP score (used for myeloid cells).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with Type A negative blood is accidentally transfused with Type A positive blood. The patient has been pregnant twice before but has never received a transfusion. What is the most likely consequence?
questions.explanation: The patient is Rh-negative and was likely sensitized to the D antigen during previous pregnancies. Transfusing Rh-positive blood triggers a secondary immune response (IgG), leading to a delayed hemolytic reaction. Option A is for IgM-mediated reactions (ABO). Option B is incorrect because the Rh mismatch matters in sensitized individuals. Option D is related to IgA deficiency. Option E involves immunocompromised recipients and viable lymphocytes.

options.B: No reaction, as the ABO group matches
options.C: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction due to anti-D antibodies

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification for her anemia?
questions.explanation:
The normal range for MCV is 80 to 100 fL. A value of 72 fL signifies microcytosis. In most microcytic anemias, there is also a decrease in hemoglobin concentration per cell, leading to hypochromia. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because normocytic anemia requires an MCV between 80-100 fL. 
C is incorrect because megaloblastic anemia presents with an MCV greater than 100 fL. 
D is incorrect because non-megaloblastic macrocytic anemia also has a high MCV. 
E is incorrect because microcytic anemias are almost always hypochromic due to decreased hemoglobin synthesis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production and differentiation of red blood cell progenitors in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation:
Erythropoietin is a glycoprotein hormone produced mainly by the kidneys that acts on erythroid progenitors (CFU-E) to increase red cell production in response to hypoxia. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because thrombopoietin stimulates platelet production. 
B is incorrect because G-CSF specifically stimulates neutrophil production. 
D is incorrect because IL-11 is used to increase platelet counts. 
E is incorrect because GM-CSF has a broader effect on both granulocyte and macrophage lines.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear examination of a patient with Vitamin B12 deficiency reveals large oval-shaped red cells and hypersegmented neutrophils. How many nuclear lobes must a neutrophil have to be classified as hypersegmented?
questions.explanation:
Hypersegmented neutrophils are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemias (B12 or Folate deficiency). They are defined as neutrophils having 5 or more distinct nuclear lobes. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because 2 lobes are seen in immature or Pelger-Huet cells. 
B is incorrect because 3-4 lobes are normal for a mature neutrophil. 
C is incorrect because while 4 is common, the diagnostic threshold for hypersegmentation is 5. 
E is incorrect because 3 lobes are a standard normal finding.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with a worm infestation. A complete blood count is performed. Which of the following reactive white blood cell changes is most likely to be seen?
questions.explanation:
Eosinophilia is characteristically associated with parasitic infections (helminthic infestations) and allergic reactions. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because neutrophilia is typically a response to acute bacterial infections or tissue necrosis. 
B is incorrect because lymphocytosis is common in viral infections like infectious mononucleosis. 
D is incorrect because monocytosis is seen in chronic infections like tuberculosis or malaria. 
E is incorrect because basophilia is rare and usually associated with myeloproliferative neoplasms.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group O will possess which of the following in their serum?
questions.explanation:
Individuals with blood group O lack both A and B antigens on their red cells. Consequently, they produce naturally occurring IgM antibodies against both A and B antigens. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because that would describe blood group B. 
B is incorrect because that would describe blood group A. 
D is incorrect because that describes blood group AB. 
E is incorrect because Rh antibodies are not naturally occurring; they require prior exposure.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia worldwide?
questions.explanation:
Iron deficiency anemia is the most prevalent form of nutritional deficiency and the leading cause of anemia globally, characterized by microcytic hypochromic indices. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because folate deficiency causes macrocytic anemia. 
B is incorrect because B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia. 
D is incorrect because lead poisoning is a cause but much less frequent than iron deficiency. 
E is incorrect because aplastic anemia is typically normocytic and normochromic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A clinician suspects a defect in the extrinsic pathway of coagulation. Which laboratory test should be ordered as the first-line investigation?
questions.explanation:
Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways of coagulation. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because bleeding time assesses platelet function and vascular integrity. 
B is incorrect because APTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways. 
D is incorrect because thrombin time specifically assesses the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. 
E is incorrect because platelet count measures quantity, not the coagulation factor cascade.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient receiving chemotherapy develops severe neutropenia. The oncologist prescribes Filgrastim (G-CSF). What is the most common clinical adverse effect associated with this growth factor?
questions.explanation:
Filgrastim (G-CSF) stimulates the bone marrow to produce white blood cells. The rapid expansion of marrow elements often leads to medullary pressure, manifesting clinically as bone pain. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because hypertension is an adverse effect more commonly linked to Erythropoietin. 
C is incorrect because thrombosis is a risk with Erythropoietin or Thrombopoietin mimetics. 
D is incorrect because pure red cell aplasia is a rare immune complication of erythropoietin therapy. 
E is incorrect because diarrhea is not a specific or common side effect of G-CSF.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by a decrease in all three cell lines (RBCs, WBCs, and platelets) in the peripheral blood?
questions.explanation:
Pancytopenia refers to the simultaneous reduction in red cells, white cells, and platelets, often seen in bone marrow failure states like aplastic anemia. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because polycythemia is an increase in red cell mass. 
B is incorrect because leukemia is a malignancy involving white cell precursors. 
D is incorrect because leukocytosis is an increase in white cell count. 
E is incorrect because thrombocytosis is an increase in platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of Hepcidin in iron metabolism?
questions.explanation:
Hepcidin is the master regulator of iron. It binds to ferroportin (the iron export channel) on enterocytes and macrophages, causing its internalization and degradation, thus trapping iron inside cells. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because hepcidin decreases iron absorption. 
B is incorrect because hepcidin has no direct effect on erythropoietin. 
D is incorrect because high hepcidin levels lead to low serum iron and transferrin saturation. 
E is incorrect because hepcidin limits iron availability for heme synthesis.

options.A: Increases iron absorption from the gut
options.C: Inhibits iron release by binding to ferroportin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male presents with a very high white cell count of 55,000/microL. The physician needs to differentiate between a Leukemoid reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). Which of the following findings strongly favors a Leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation:
A Leukemoid reaction is an exaggerated white cell response to infection. Neutrophils in this state are functionally mature and have high LAP enzyme activity. In contrast, CML cells are neoplastic and typically have a very low or absent LAP score. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because the Philadelphia chromosome is the hallmark of CML. 
B is incorrect because a low LAP score is characteristic of CML. 
D is incorrect because blasts suggest acute leukemia, though some may appear in CML; they are rare in leukemoid reactions. 
E is incorrect because massive splenomegaly is a classic sign of CML, not a leukemoid reaction.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An elderly patient with chronic gastritis presents with neurological symptoms and macrocytic anemia. Laboratory tests confirm Vitamin B12 deficiency. What is the most likely underlying mechanism if this is Pernicious Anemia?
questions.explanation:
Pernicious anemia is a specific form of B12 deficiency caused by an autoimmune attack on gastric parietal cells or intrinsic factor (IF) itself, leading to a failure of B12 absorption in the ileum. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because folate is found in vegetables; B12 is in animal products. 
B is incorrect because while Celiac causes malabsorption, Pernicious Anemia specifically involves the stomach/IF. 
D is incorrect because pregnancy usually causes folate deficiency rather than B12. 
E is incorrect because Diphyllobothrium latum is a cause of B12 deficiency but is not called Pernicious Anemia.

options.A: Dietary insufficiency of green leafy vegetables

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with spontaneous mucosal bleeding and petechiae. The lab results show a prolonged bleeding time but a normal PT and APTT. The platelet count is 20,000/microL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
Petechiae and mucosal bleeding are signs of primary hemostatic defects (platelets). ITP involves autoimmune destruction of platelets, leading to low counts and prolonged bleeding time, but coagulation factors (PT/APTT) remain normal. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because Hemophilia A presents with normal bleeding time and prolonged APTT. 
B is incorrect because Vitamin K deficiency affects factors II, VII, IX, and X, prolonging PT and APTT. 
D is incorrect because vWD usually has a normal platelet count (except Type 2B). 
E is incorrect because DIC would show prolonged PT, APTT, and decreased fibrinogen.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with microcytic anemia, which laboratory parameter is most useful to differentiate between Iron Deficiency Anemia and Thalassemia Minor when both show low MCV?
questions.explanation:
In Iron Deficiency Anemia, the RDW is typically increased because of significant variation in cell size (anisocytosis). In Thalassemia Minor, the cells are uniformly small, so the RDW is usually normal. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because hemoglobin is low in both. 
C is incorrect because TLC is usually normal in both uncomplicated conditions. 
D is incorrect because hematocrit is reduced in both. 
E is incorrect because MCH is decreased in both microcytic anemias.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with Iron Deficiency Anemia. Which of the following sets of iron studies is most consistent with this diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
In iron deficiency, stores (ferritin) are depleted first. Serum iron falls, and the liver compensates by increasing transferrin production (measured as Total Iron Binding Capacity - TIBC) to capture more iron. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because it describes Anemia of Chronic Disease. 
C is incorrect because it suggests Hemochromatosis/Iron overload. 
D is incorrect because iron stores must be low and serum iron usually follows. 
E is incorrect because it is physiologically inconsistent for iron deficiency.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, High Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old male with a history of recurrent painful joint swelling (hemarthrosis) since childhood undergoes preoperative screening. His PT is 12 seconds (Normal: 11-13) and APTT is 75 seconds (Normal: 25-35). Bleeding time is normal. A mixing study with normal plasma corrects the APTT to 30 seconds. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
Hemarthrosis is a classic sign of secondary hemostasis defects. Normal PT and prolonged APTT indicate a defect in the intrinsic pathway (Factors VIII, IX, XI, XII). Correction with a mixing study suggests a factor deficiency rather than an inhibitor. Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) is the most common cause fitting this clinical and lab profile. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because vWD usually prolongs bleeding time and only mildly prolongs APTT. 
C is incorrect because Factor VII deficiency would prolong PT, not APTT. 
D is incorrect because APS is an inhibitor state; the APTT would not correct with a mixing study. 
E is incorrect because scurvy (Vit C) causes capillary fragility and prolonged bleeding time.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient develops sudden fever, chills, and hypotension 15 minutes after the start of a packed red blood cell transfusion. His urine appears dark red. What is the immunological basis of this Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction?
questions.explanation:
Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are usually due to ABO incompatibility. Pre-formed IgM antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to donor A or B antigens, causing immediate intravascular hemolysis via the classical complement pathway. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because IgE mediates allergic/urticarial reactions. 
C is incorrect because delayed reactions are usually IgG mediated (anamnestic response) and occur days later. 
D is incorrect because donor anti-HLA antibodies mediate TRALI (Transfusion Related Acute Lung Injury). 
E is incorrect because IgA deficiency leads to anaphylactic reactions, not hemolysis.

options.D: Anti-HLA antibodies in the donor plasma

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male with a history of weight loss and bone pain shows normocytic anemia on peripheral smear. The bone marrow biopsy reveals "Dry Tap," and the trephine biopsy shows the marrow space replaced by fibrous tissue and nests of metastatic cells. What is the name of this specific type of anemia?
questions.explanation:
Myelophthisic anemia is a form of marrow failure where the hematopoietic space is physically replaced (infiltrated) by tumors, fibrosis, or granulomas. This often causes a "dry tap" on aspiration and a leukoerythroblastic picture on peripheral smear. 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because sideroblastic anemia is a defect in heme synthesis with ring sideroblasts. 
B is incorrect because aplastic anemia is primary marrow hypocellularity, not replacement by abnormal tissue. 
D is incorrect because megaloblastic anemia involves DNA synthesis defects. 
E is incorrect because hemolytic anemia involves premature destruction of RBCs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An infant is born with severe jaundice and hydrops fetalis. The mother is Rh-negative and the father is Rh-positive. This is her second pregnancy. What is the mechanism of the destruction of fetal red cells in Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn?
questions.explanation:
Rh incompatibility causes HDN when an Rh-negative mother is sensitized to Rh-positive fetal cells (usually in a first pregnancy). In subsequent pregnancies, maternal IgG (which can cross the placenta) coats fetal Rh-positive cells, leading to their destruction by splenic macrophages (extravascular hemolysis). 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because IgM cannot cross the placenta. 
C is incorrect because IgA does not mediate this reaction. 
D is incorrect because bilirubin is a product of hemolysis, not the cause. 
E is incorrect because the bone marrow is not the primary site of damage.

options.B: Extravascular hemolysis by IgG antibodies crossing the placenta
options.D: Direct toxic effect of bilirubin on fetal RBCs
options.E: Failure of fetal bone marrow due to maternal steroids

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 19-year-old college student presents with sore throat, fever, and generalized lymphadenopathy. Peripheral blood film shows 60 percent lymphocytes, many of which are large with abundant "ballerina-skirt" cytoplasm indenting around adjacent RBCs. Which cell type is represented by these findings?
questions.explanation:
The description is classic for Infectious Mononucleosis. The "atypical lymphocytes" seen on the smear are actually reactive effector T-cells (mostly CD8+) responding to B-cells infected by the Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV). 
Debunking: 
A is incorrect because lymphoblasts have high N:C ratios and fine chromatin, not abundant flowing cytoplasm. 
C is incorrect because mature resting lymphocytes are small with minimal cytoplasm. 
D is incorrect because plasma cells have an eccentric nucleus and a perinuclear clear zone (hof). 
E is incorrect because NK cells are large granular lymphocytes but do not typically show the "skirt" morphology.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25 year old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which category of anemia does this represent?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. Option A is incorrect because macrocytic anemia requires an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option B is incorrect as normocytic anemia ranges from 80 to 100 fL. Option D is incorrect because hyperchromic is not a standard morphological classification as the cell cannot be overfilled with hemoglobin. Option E is a subset of macrocytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood film shows hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is highly characteristic of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic feature of megaloblastic anemia due to Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. Option A, C, D, and E are incorrect as these typically present with normal neutrophil morphology or different red cell abnormalities (like microcytosis in IDA and Thalassemia).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary clinical use of recombinant Erythropoietin therapy?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin is mainly produced in the kidneys; hence, patients with chronic kidney disease develop deficiency and anemia, making them the primary candidates for this therapy. Option A, D, and E are incorrect as EPO does not treat malignancies, infections, or clotting factor deficiencies. Option C is incorrect because EPO can actually increase the risk of thrombosis as an adverse effect.

options.B: Treatment of anemia in chronic kidney disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is considered a benign cause of reactive leukocytosis involving eosinophils?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is commonly seen in parasitic infections and allergic reactions. Option A is incorrect as viral infections often cause lymphocytosis. Option C is incorrect as pyogenic infections cause neutrophilia. Option D and E are incorrect because nutritional anemias do not typically cause reactive eosinophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory parameter is used to assess the average weight of hemoglobin in an individual red blood cell?
questions.explanation: MCH measures the average content (weight) of hemoglobin per red cell. Option A is incorrect as it measures volume (size). Option C is incorrect as it measures variation in cell size. Option D is incorrect as it measures the volume percentage of RBCs in blood. Option E is incorrect as it measures bone marrow activity.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which blood group individual is known as the universal donor for packed red blood cell transfusion?
questions.explanation: O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe for transfusion into almost any recipient in emergencies. Option C is incorrect as AB positive is the universal recipient. Option E is incorrect because O positive cells can trigger a reaction in Rh-negative recipients. Options A and B are restricted by antigen compatibility.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bleeding time is a laboratory test primarily used to evaluate the function of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Bleeding time assesses the formation of the primary platelet plug. Option A and B are assessed by aPTT and PT respectively. Option D is incorrect as it is part of the common pathway and assessed by Thrombin Time. Option E is incorrect as it is a natural anticoagulant.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which cell type in the bone marrow is the direct precursor that fragments to produce platelets?
questions.explanation: Megakaryocytes undergo cytoplasmic fragmentation to release platelets into the circulation. Option A, B, C, and E are precursors for neutrophils, lymphocytes, erythrocytes, and monocytes respectively.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old male with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents with a sudden onset of fever, chills, and lower back pain during a blood transfusion. His urine appears dark red. What is the most likely mechanism of this reaction?
questions.explanation: This is an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, usually due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies fix complement, causing intravascular hemolysis. Option A is incorrect as it describes an allergic/anaphylactic reaction (urticaria). Option B describes a febrile non-hemolytic reaction. Option D describes a delayed hemolytic reaction. Option E is a chronic complication, not an acute one.

options.A: IgE mediated hypersensitivity to plasma proteins
options.B: Preformed antibodies reacting against donor leucocytes
options.C: Preformed IgM antibodies causing complement-mediated hemolysis
options.D: Delayed T-cell mediated response against Rh antigens
options.E: Iron overload due to chronic transfusions

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following sets of laboratory findings is most consistent with a diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia?
questions.explanation: In IDA, iron stores are depleted (Low Ferritin), leading to low circulating iron. The body increases transferrin production to compensate, leading to High Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC). Option A and E are incorrect as they resemble anemia of chronic disease or other patterns. Option C resembles hemochromatosis. Option D is physiologically inconsistent.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12 year old boy presents with repeated episodes of joint swelling (hemarthrosis) after minor trauma. Laboratory tests show a prolonged activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) and a normal Prothrombin Time (PT). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) causes isolated aPTT prolongation and presents with deep tissue bleeding like hemarthrosis. Option A, D, and E would typically show an elevated PT because Factor VII (extrinsic pathway) has the shortest half-life and is affected first. Option B usually presents with mucosal bleeding and may have a normal or slightly prolonged aPTT, but rarely causes hemarthrosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis with a shift to the left (increase in band cells) is most commonly associated with which of the following?
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections trigger the release of mature and immature (bands) neutrophils from the bone marrow. Option A and D cause lymphocytosis (often with atypical lymphocytes). Option C is a malignancy, not a reactive process. Option E causes eosinophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is receiving Filgrastim (G-CSF) after chemotherapy. Which of the following is a recognized adverse effect of this hematopoietic growth factor?
questions.explanation: Bone pain is a common side effect of G-CSF due to the rapid expansion of hematopoietic cells in the marrow. Option A is a side effect of Erythropoietin. Option C is a rare side effect of EPO (anti-EPO antibodies). Option D and E are toxicities of specific chemotherapy drugs (Cyclophosphamide and Bleomycin), not growth factors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow examination of a patient with severe anemia reveals increased cellularity with many large, immature-looking erythroid precursors showing nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony (immature nuclei with mature pink cytoplasm). What is the underlying biochemical defect?
questions.explanation: Nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony is the hallmark of megaloblastic anemia, caused by impaired DNA synthesis (due to B12/Folate deficiency) while RNA/protein synthesis in the cytoplasm continues. Option A describes microcytic anemias like IDA. Option C describes hemolytic anemias. Option D describes Thalassemia/Sickle cell. Option E describes aplastic anemia.

options.C: Premature destruction of RBCs in the spleen

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old patient with chronic renal failure is on Erythropoietin therapy. He suddenly develops a worsening of his blood pressure and a thrombotic stroke. Which mechanism best explains the thrombotic risk associated with this therapy?
questions.explanation: EPO increases the hematocrit; when it rises too high or too quickly, blood viscosity increases significantly, predisposing to hypertension and thrombosis. Option A, C, D, and E are not the primary established mechanisms for EPO-induced thrombosis.

options.A: Direct activation of the coagulation cascade by EPO
options.B: Increase in blood viscosity due to high red cell mass
options.C: Inhibition of Protein C and S synthesis
options.D: Destabilization of atherosclerotic plaques by EPO
options.E: Decrease in nitric oxide production by endothelium

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: How is Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD) best differentiated from Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA) on the basis of laboratory findings?
questions.explanation: In ACD, iron is trapped inside macrophages due to high hepcidin; thus, Ferritin (storage iron) is normal or high. In IDA, total body iron is low, so Ferritin is low. Option A is inverted; IDA has high TIBC. Option C is incorrect; both are usually microcytic/normocytic. Option D is incorrect; IDA has absent marrow iron. Option E is inverted; hepcidin is the mediator of ACD.

options.A: ACD has high TIBC, while IDA has low TIBC
options.B: ACD has high serum ferritin, while IDA has low serum ferritin
options.C: ACD shows macrocytosis, while IDA shows microcytosis
options.D: ACD has increased marrow iron, while IDA has increased serum iron
options.E: ACD has low hepcidin levels, while IDA has high hepcidin levels

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the context of peripheral blood stem cell transplantation, G-CSF is used to mobilize CD34+ stem cells from the bone marrow into the peripheral blood. What is the molecular mechanism behind this mobilization?
questions.explanation: G-CSF induces the release of proteases (like elastase) from neutrophils which cleave adhesion molecules (like VCAM-1 and SDF-1) that anchor stem cells to the marrow stroma, allowing them to escape into the blood. Options A, C, D, and E do not accurately describe the mobilization process.

options.A: Direct stimulation of stem cell motility
options.B: Proteolytic cleavage of adhesion molecules like VCAM-1
options.C: Destruction of the bone marrow stroma
options.D: Increasing the permeability of marrow sinusoids

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old female presents with menorrhagia and easy bruising. Her labs show a normal platelet count, prolonged Bleeding Time, and a slightly prolonged aPTT. Her Factor VIII levels are low, but PT is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: vWD is unique because it affects both platelet adhesion (Prolonged Bleeding Time) and the intrinsic pathway (vWF stabilizes Factor VIII, so deficiency leads to low Factor VIII and prolonged aPTT). Option A and B would have a normal Bleeding Time. Option D would have low platelets and prolonged PT. Option E would have a low platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with fatigue and pallor. Her laboratory report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification for her anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: A Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) below 80 fL (normal range 80-100 fL) defines microcytic anemia, which is almost always associated with low Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), making it hypochromic. Debunking: A is incorrect as macrocytic implies MCV over 100 fL. C is incorrect as normocytic implies MCV of 80-100 fL. D is incorrect because hyperchromic anemias are rare and would have high MCV. E is incorrect as normocytic refers to normal size.

options.A: Macrocytic normochromic anemia [B] Microcytic hypochromic anemia [C] Normocytic normochromic anemia [D] Macrocytic hyperchromic anemia [E] Normocytic hypochromic anemia
options.B: Microcytic hypochromic anemia [C] Normocytic normochromic anemia [D] Macrocytic hyperchromic anemia [E] Normocytic hypochromic anemia
options.C: Normocytic normochromic anemia [D] Macrocytic hyperchromic anemia [E] Normocytic hypochromic anemia
options.D: Macrocytic hyperchromic anemia [E] Normocytic hypochromic anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a vital hematopoietic growth factor. What is the primary site of its production in an adult human?
questions.explanation: Logic: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is synthesized by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Debunking: A is incorrect as the liver is the primary site in the fetus but only a minor site in adults. B is incorrect as the spleen is involved in blood cell destruction, not EPO production. D is incorrect because the bone marrow is the target organ, not the producer of EPO. E is incorrect as the lungs sense oxygen but do not produce EPO.

options.A: Liver [B] Spleen [C] Peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney [D] Bone marrow stroma [E] Pulmonary alveolar cells
options.B: Spleen [C] Peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney [D] Bone marrow stroma [E] Pulmonary alveolar cells
options.C: Peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney [D] Bone marrow stroma [E] Pulmonary alveolar cells
options.D: Bone marrow stroma [E] Pulmonary alveolar cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear of a patient shows a large number of red cells with an increased central zone of pallor, occupying more than one-third of the cell diameter. This finding is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Logic: Increased central pallor defines hypochromia, which is a hallmark of iron deficiency anemia due to impaired hemoglobin synthesis. Debunking: A and B are incorrect because megaloblastic anemias (B12/Folate) are macrocytic and normochromic. D is incorrect as spherocytes lack central pallor entirely. E is incorrect because acute blood loss initially presents as normocytic normochromic anemia.

options.A: Vitamin B12 deficiency [B] Folate deficiency [C] Iron deficiency [D] Hereditary spherocytosis [E] Acute blood loss
options.B: Folate deficiency [C] Iron deficiency [D] Hereditary spherocytosis [E] Acute blood loss
options.C: Iron deficiency [D] Hereditary spherocytosis [E] Acute blood loss

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is specifically used to reduce the duration and severity of neutropenia in patients receiving cytotoxic chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: Logic: G-CSF (Filgrastim) stimulates the production and maturation of neutrophils, making it the standard treatment for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. Debunking: A is used for red cell production. B is for platelet production. D (Oprelvekin) is used for thrombocytopenia. E is a broad-acting factor not specifically used for neutropenia clinical management.

options.A: Erythropoietin [B] Thrombopoietin [C] Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) [D] Interleukin-11 [E] Stem cell factor
options.B: Thrombopoietin [C] Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) [D] Interleukin-11 [E] Stem cell factor
options.C: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) [D] Interleukin-11 [E] Stem cell factor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, which blood group is considered the universal donor for Packed Red Blood Cells (PRBCs)?
questions.explanation: Logic: Group O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens on the red cell surface, meaning it will not be lysed by recipient antibodies. Debunking: A and B have antigens that would react with anti-A or anti-B antibodies. C is the universal recipient for cells but not a donor. E is incorrect because the Rh positive factor can cause reactions in Rh negative recipients.

options.A: Group A positive [B] Group B negative [C] Group AB positive [D] Group O negative [E] Group O positive
options.B: Group B negative [C] Group AB positive [D] Group O negative [E] Group O positive
options.C: Group AB positive [D] Group O negative [E] Group O positive

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with abnormal bleeding after a minor surgery. The laboratory reports a prolonged Prothrombin Time (PT) but a normal Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). This pattern indicates a deficiency in which coagulation factor?
questions.explanation: Logic: PT measures the extrinsic and common pathways. Factor VII is the only factor unique to the extrinsic pathway. Debunking: A, B, D, and E are all part of the intrinsic pathway, which would result in a prolonged APTT, not a prolonged PT with a normal APTT.

options.A: Factor VIII [B] Factor IX [C] Factor VII [D] Factor XII [E] Factor XI
options.B: Factor IX [C] Factor VII [D] Factor XII [E] Factor XI

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by a reactive increase in the number of circulating neutrophils, often accompanied by a left shift?
questions.explanation: Logic: Bacterial infections typically trigger a neutrophilic leukocytosis. A left shift refers to the release of immature forms like band cells. Debunking: A usually causes lymphocytosis. C and D typically cause eosinophilia. E is a malignant proliferation of lymphocytes, not a reactive neutrophilia.

options.A: Viral infection [B] Acute bacterial infection [C] Parasitic infestation [D] Bronchial asthma [E] Chronic lymphocytic leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is primarily caused by an impairment in the synthesis of:
questions.explanation: Logic: Vitamin B12 and Folate are essential co-factors for thymidine synthesis; their deficiency leads to defective DNA replication and nuclear-cytoplasmic dyssynchrony. Debunking: A is impaired in sideroblastic anemia. B is impaired in thalassemias. D is impaired in hereditary spherocytosis. E is not the primary mechanism of megaloblastic changes.

options.A: Heme [B] Globin chains [C] DNA [D] Cell membrane proteins [E] Erythropoietin receptors

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Thrombocytopenia is clinically defined as a platelet count below which threshold?
questions.explanation: Logic: The normal platelet count ranges from 150,000 to 450,000/microL. Any value below 150,000 is defined as thrombocytopenia. Debunking: A and B are within the normal range. D and E represent severe thrombocytopenia where bleeding risk increases significantly, but they are not the starting definition threshold.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, the primary mechanism of red cell destruction is:
questions.explanation: Logic: Acute reactions usually involve ABO incompatibility where pre-existing IgM antibodies fix complement, leading to immediate intravascular hemolysis. Debunking: A describes G6PD deficiency. C describes extravascular hemolysis (like Rh incompatibility). D and E are not the standard mechanisms for acute hemolytic transfusion reactions.

questions.explanation: Logic: Alcoholism often leads to folate deficiency, resulting in megaloblastic anemia. The marrow shows hypercellularity due to erythroid hyperplasia, with large immature-looking erythroid precursors (megaloblasts). Debunking: A is characteristic of aplastic anemia. C is seen in sideroblastic anemia. D is seen in myelofibrosis. E is the definition of acute leukemia.

questions.explanation: Logic: Erythropoietin therapy is frequently associated with an increase in blood pressure (hypertension) and sometimes thrombotic complications due to increased blood viscosity. Debunking: A is wrong because it causes high, not low, BP. B is wrong as EPO uses up iron stores, often causing iron deficiency. D and E are not recognized primary side effects of EPO therapy.

questions.explanation: Logic: vWD affects both platelet adhesion (prolonging Bleeding Time) and carries Factor VIII (prolonging APTT). Platelet count and PT remain normal. Debunking: A and B only prolong APTT; Bleeding Time is normal because platelet function is intact. D and E would typically prolong the PT.

questions.question: A patient is found to have a Leukemoid Reaction due to a severe underlying infection. Which of the following laboratory findings helps distinguish this from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)?
questions.explanation: Logic: In reactive leukocytosis (Leukemoid reaction), the neutrophils are functionally mature and have high alkaline phosphatase levels. In CML, the malignant cells have low LAP scores. Debunking: A, C, and D are characteristic of CML, not a Leukemoid reaction. E is common in CML but rare in simple reactive leukocytosis.

questions.question: A patient with suspected anemia has the following indices: Hb 9 g/dL, MCV 75 fL, MCHC 30 g/dL, Serum Iron low, and Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) high. These findings are most consistent with:
questions.explanation: Logic: Low iron with a high TIBC is the classic biochemical profile of iron deficiency. The TIBC increases as the body attempts to capture more iron. Debunking: A has normal TIBC. B has low TIBC and low iron (iron is trapped in stores). D and E involve high iron levels or normal TIBC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male has mild jaundice and splenomegaly. His lab work shows normocytic anemia with a Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) of 38 g/dL (Normal: 32-36 g/dL). Peripheral smear shows small, dark red cells lacking central pallor. What is the underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Logic: High MCHC is a specific clue for Hereditary Spherocytosis. Loss of membrane leads to cell shrinkage and concentration of hemoglobin. Debunking: A causes macrocytosis. B causes microcytosis. D is the mechanism of Sickle Cell Anemia. E leads to Heinz bodies and bite cells during oxidative stress.

options.A: Defective DNA synthesis [B] Defective heme synthesis [C] Mutations in red cell membrane proteins (Spectrin/Ankyrin) [D] Substitution of valine for glutamic acid in globin chain [E] Deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase
options.B: Defective heme synthesis [C] Mutations in red cell membrane proteins (Spectrin/Ankyrin) [D] Substitution of valine for glutamic acid in globin chain [E] Deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase
options.C: Mutations in red cell membrane proteins (Spectrin/Ankyrin) [D] Substitution of valine for glutamic acid in globin chain [E] Deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase
options.D: Substitution of valine for glutamic acid in globin chain [E] Deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient requires a blood transfusion but has a history of multiple previous transfusions. Minutes after the start of a new unit, he develops fever, chills, and dark-colored urine. Laboratory tests confirm hemoglobinuria and a positive direct Coombs test. What is the most likely immunological basis for this reaction?
questions.explanation: Logic: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are Type II cytotoxic hypersensitivity reactions where antibodies (usually IgM) bind to donor RBC antigens and fix complement. Debunking: A is allergic/anaphylactic. C involves immune complexes. D is cell-mediated (delayed). E involves donor T-cells attacking the host.

options.A: Type I Hypersensitivity [B] Type II Hypersensitivity (IgM/IgG mediated complement fixation) [C] Type III Hypersensitivity [D] Type IV Hypersensitivity [E] Graft versus Host Disease
options.B: Type II Hypersensitivity (IgM/IgG mediated complement fixation) [C] Type III Hypersensitivity [D] Type IV Hypersensitivity [E] Graft versus Host Disease
options.C: Type III Hypersensitivity [D] Type IV Hypersensitivity [E] Graft versus Host Disease
options.D: Type IV Hypersensitivity [E] Graft versus Host Disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with megaloblastic anemia, a bone marrow examination is performed. Which of the following is a characteristic feature of the white cell precursors in this condition?
questions.explanation: Logic: Megaloblastic anemia affects all cell lines. White cell precursors show enlarged size (giant metamyelocytes) due to the same DNA synthesis defect seen in RBCs. Debunking: A is specific for Acute Myeloid Leukemia. C is seen in sepsis/infection. D is a specific congenital or acquired enzyme deficiency. E is characteristic of acute leukemia.

options.A: Presence of Auer rods [B] Giant metamyelocytes and band forms [C] Toxic granulations [D] Absence of myeloperoxidase [E] Shift to the left with many myeloblasts
options.B: Giant metamyelocytes and band forms [C] Toxic granulations [D] Absence of myeloperoxidase [E] Shift to the left with many myeloblasts
options.C: Toxic granulations [D] Absence of myeloperoxidase [E] Shift to the left with many myeloblasts
options.D: Absence of myeloperoxidase [E] Shift to the left with many myeloblasts
options.E: Shift to the left with many myeloblasts

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A child is investigated for microcytic anemia. The Hb is 10 g/dL, MCV is 62 fL, and the Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC count) is 11. Which investigation is most definitive to confirm the suspected diagnosis of Thalassemia Trait over Iron Deficiency Anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: A Mentzer index less than 13 suggests Thalassemia. Hb Electrophoresis is the definitive test to identify abnormal ratios of HbA2 or HbF. Debunking: A, B, D, and E are tests for iron status, which would help rule out Iron Deficiency Anemia but cannot definitively diagnose Thalassemia.

options.A: Serum Ferritin [B] Bone marrow iron stain [C] Hemoglobin electrophoresis [D] Serum Iron levels [E] Total Iron Binding Capacity
options.B: Bone marrow iron stain [C] Hemoglobin electrophoresis [D] Serum Iron levels [E] Total Iron Binding Capacity
options.C: Hemoglobin electrophoresis [D] Serum Iron levels [E] Total Iron Binding Capacity
options.D: Serum Iron levels [E] Total Iron Binding Capacity

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female has lifelong mucosal bleeding. Laboratory results show a prolonged bleeding time and a low platelet count. Platelet aggregation studies show a failure to aggregate with Ristocetin, which is NOT corrected by adding normal plasma. Which platelet glycoprotein is likely deficient?
questions.explanation: Logic: Failure to aggregate with Ristocetin that is not corrected by normal plasma (meaning the factor is present in plasma but the receptor is missing) indicates Bernard-Soulier Syndrome, a deficiency of GpIb. Debunking: A is deficient in Glanzmann thrombasthenia. C is a collagen receptor. D is an adhesion molecule on activated platelets. E is von Willebrand disease, which would correct with normal plasma.

options.A: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa [B] Glycoprotein Ib-IX-V [C] Glycoprotein VI [D] P-selectin [E] Von Willebrand Factor
options.B: Glycoprotein Ib-IX-V [C] Glycoprotein VI [D] P-selectin [E] Von Willebrand Factor
options.C: Glycoprotein VI [D] P-selectin [E] Von Willebrand Factor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the proliferation and differentiation of neutrophil progenitors?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) specifically targets the myeloid lineage to increase the production of neutrophils. Option A is incorrect because Erythropoietin primarily stimulates red blood cell production in response to hypoxia. Option B is incorrect because Thrombopoietin is the primary regulator of platelet production from megakaryocytes. Option D is incorrect because Interleukin-11 is used to stimulate platelet production and has broader systemic effects. Option E is incorrect because while GM-CSF stimulates neutrophils, it also acts on macrophages and eosinophils, making it less specific than G-CSF.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the morphological classification of an anemia with a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL and a Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) of 24 pg?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because an MCV below 80 fL defines microcytosis and an MCH below 27 pg defines hypochromia, which is characteristic of iron deficiency or thalassemia. Option B is incorrect because macrocytic anemia requires an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option C is incorrect because normocytic anemia requires an MCV between 80-100 fL. Option D is incorrect because microcytic anemias are almost invariably hypochromic due to reduced hemoglobin synthesis. Option E is incorrect because macrocytic cells usually have a normal or increased hemoglobin content per cell.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue and pica. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL. Which of the following investigations is the most sensitive early indicator of depleted total body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because serum ferritin levels directly correlate with total body iron stores and are the first to decline during the storage depletion phase of iron deficiency. Option A is incorrect because serum iron fluctuates daily and only drops after stores are exhausted. Option B is incorrect because TIBC increases as a compensatory mechanism but is less specific than ferritin. Option C is incorrect because transferrin saturation decreases only after the iron-deficient erythropoiesis phase begins. Option D is incorrect because protoporphyrin levels rise only when iron is unavailable for heme synthesis, representing a later stage.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old male with a history of total gastrectomy presents with paresthesia and macrocytic anemia. Which finding on the peripheral blood smear would most strongly support a diagnosis of megaloblastic anemia?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because hypersegmented neutrophils (more than five nuclear lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency, reflecting impaired DNA synthesis. Option A is incorrect because target cells are typically seen in thalassemias or liver disease. Option C is incorrect because schistocytes are fragmented red cells seen in microangiopathic hemolytic anemias. Option D is incorrect because basophilic stippling is associated with lead poisoning or sideroblastic anemia. Option E is incorrect because Heinz bodies represent denatured hemoglobin seen in G6PD deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following clinical conditions is a classic cause of reactive eosinophilic leukocytosis?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because eosinophilia is a characteristic reactive response to allergic conditions like asthma, hay fever, and parasitic infestations. Option A is incorrect because viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. Option B is incorrect because pyogenic bacteria elicit a neutrophilic response. Option C is incorrect because Chronic Myeloid Leukemia is a malignant neoplastic proliferation, not a reactive process. Option E is incorrect because typhoid fever is classically associated with leucopenia and relative lymphocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Malignant disorders of white blood cells are broadly classified into two major categories based on the lineage of the neoplastic cell. What are these two categories?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the primary classification of hematological malignancies is based on whether the neoplastic clone arises from the myeloid progenitor (giving rise to granulocytes, RBCs, platelets) or the lymphoid progenitor (B, T, and NK cells). Option B is incorrect because "benign" is not a standard classification for WBC malignancies, which are inherently aggressive. Option C is incorrect because while it describes cell types, it is not the formal pathological classification system. Option D is incorrect because these terms refer to the duration of inflammation, not the lineage of malignancy. Option E is incorrect because these terms describe the location of hematopoiesis, not the type of neoplasm.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 6-year-old boy presents with spontaneous bleeding into his right knee joint (hemarthrosis). His laboratory profile shows a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT), but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT), normal bleeding time, and normal platelet count. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) characteristically presents with isolated prolongation of the APTT (intrinsic pathway) and deep tissue bleeding like hemarthrosis, while platelet function (bleeding time) remains normal. Option A is incorrect because Von Willebrand Disease usually presents with a prolonged bleeding time due to defective platelet adhesion. Option B is incorrect because Vitamin K deficiency affects Factors II, VII, IX, and X, typically prolonging both PT and APTT. Option D is incorrect because ITP presents with a low platelet count and prolonged bleeding time. Option E is incorrect because DIC involves consumption of all factors, prolonging PT, APTT, and bleeding time while lowering platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood group system, an individual with blood group 'O' is characterized by which of the following?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because blood group O individuals lack both A and B agglutinogens (antigens) on the surface of their erythrocytes. Option A is incorrect because the presence of both antigens defines blood group AB. Option B is incorrect because group O individuals possess both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their serum. Option C is incorrect because the absence of antibodies is characteristic of group AB. Option D is incorrect because while H substance is the precursor, the A antigen is absent in group O.

options.A: Presence of A and B antigens on RBCs
options.B: Presence of only A antibodies in serum
options.C: Absence of A and B antibodies in serum
options.D: Presence of H substance and A antigen
options.E: Absence of both A and B antigens on RBCs

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient experiences an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction due to an ABO incompatibility. What is the primary mechanism of red cell destruction in this emergency?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because ABO incompatibility involves pre-existing IgM antibodies that fix complement, leading to the formation of the Membrane Attack Complex (MAC) and immediate intravascular hemolysis. Option A is incorrect because T-cell responses are too slow for an acute reaction. Option C is incorrect because splenic sequestration (extravascular hemolysis) is more typical of Rh incompatibility or minor blood group mismatches. Option D is incorrect because ADCC is not the dominant mechanism in acute ABO reactions. Option E is incorrect because this describes Type III hypersensitivity, whereas acute hemolysis is Type II.

options.D: Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity by NK cells
options.E: Formation of immune complexes in the joints

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 40-year-old patient presents with severe anemia and a reticulocyte count of 0.1%. A bone marrow aspiration is performed, showing "dry tap," and the subsequent biopsy reveals hypocellularity with replacement of hematopoietic tissue by fat cells. This finding is most diagnostic of:
